title: The Organ Loyalty Programs of Private Healthcare
script: Right. So by 2048, there were three ways to get a new kidney. You could grow one in a lab. Perfect match, zero rejection risk, roughly the cost of a small house. You could get a pig kidney, edited with CRISPR to be compatible with human biology. Cheap, effective, and came with a lingering social stigma that never quite went away. Or you could wait for a traditional donor organ, which by this point was about as rare as finding a vinyl record shop. An American insurance company called HealthFutura looked at this landscape and thought: loyalty program. For two hundred and ninety-nine dollars a month, subscribers could pre-register for bioprinted replacement organs and earn what HealthFutura cheerfully called Organ Points. Gold tier members received priority access to kidneys. Platinum members could request custom-grown hearts with cosmetic modifications. And yes, people genuinely ordered heart-shaped hearts. The company's free tier offered pig organs. With a ninety-day return policy. For organs. The marketing slogan was 'Because You Deserve Human Parts,' which is one of those sentences that sounds perfectly fine until you think about it for more than about four seconds. What actually happened, predictably, was the creation of a two-tier system that mirrored every other two-tier system in American life. Wealthy subscribers received bespoke organs grown from their own stem cells. Biologically perfect, socially prestigious. Everyone else got pig organs that functioned identically but carried an invisible mark. 'Are you bio or pig?' became a whispered question at dinner parties. It functioned identically to asking someone their salary, but somehow felt more invasive. The return policy, incidentally, was quietly discontinued in 2050 after seventeen people attempted to return functioning pig kidneys for a bioprinted upgrade. One man mailed his back.

open_loops.text: three ways to get a kidney
pig organs with a return policy

but_therefore_transitions: HealthFutura looked at this landscape and thought: loyalty program (THEREFORE)
everyone else got pig organs that functioned identically BUT carried an invisible mark
the return policy was quietly discontinued (THEREFORE — consequence of absurd premise)

scenes.prompt: Hyper-realistic photograph of a hospital lobby circa 2048. The space has holographic blue displays showing patient queues but the chairs are slightly worn, the floor has visible foot traffic patterns, and a hand sanitizer station has a smudged touchscreen. A faded corporate sign reads HEALTHFUTURA YOUR ORGAN YOUR WAY. Nervous patients in modern casual clothing sit waiting, one scrolling a phone with a cracked screen. Mixed fluorescent and natural lighting, slightly cluttered reception desk. Lived-in environment, realistic wear and tear. A hyper-realistic medical laboratory circa 2048. A translucent bioprinting chamber glows soft blue, slowly assembling a human kidney layer by layer from living tissue. A tired-looking lab technician in a slightly wrinkled white coat watches the process while drinking coffee from a stained mug. The lab has advanced equipment but also cluttered desks, sticky notes on monitors, and a motivational poster peeling at one corner. The chamber rotates gently revealing the growing organ. Warm fluorescent lighting mixed with blue glow. Lived-in lab environment with realistic clutter. Hyper-realistic photograph of a medical returns counter at a hospital, circa 2048. A bewildered receptionist in slightly rumpled hospital scrubs stares at a small medical cooler placed on a worn counter by a determined-looking middle-aged man. Behind them a printed sign taped to the wall reads ORGAN EXCHANGES 90 DAY POLICY with a coffee ring stain on the bottom corner. Fluorescent lighting, scuffed linoleum floor, a pen jar with mismatched pens. The receptionists expression is somewhere between horror and bureaucratic resignation. A middle-aged man in casual slightly wrinkled clothing stands at a weathered blue postal mailbox on a suburban street, circa 2048. He carefully places a small padded medical shipping box labeled FRAGILE BIOLOGICAL MATERIAL into the mailbox slot. He dusts off his hands with quiet satisfaction and walks away casually. The suburban street looks like today but with electric vehicles and a delivery drone with faded paint hovering in the background. Afternoon sunlight, patched sidewalk, slightly overgrown lawn next door.
